pairs.question: What is Bute House, and who does it serve as the official residence and workplace for?
pairs.answer: Bute House is the official residence and workplace of the First Minister of Scotland, located at 6 Charlotte Square in Edinburgh's New Town.

pairs.question: Who designed Bute House and the surrounding Charlotte Square?
pairs.answer: Bute House was designed by Scottish architect Robert Adam, who was also commissioned to design the unified frontages for Charlotte Square as part of James Craig's 1767 'New Town plan'.

pairs.question: How did Bute House become the official residence of the First Minister of Scotland?
pairs.answer: Bute House was conveyed to the National Trust for Scotland by the 6th Marquess of Bute in 1966 and later served as the residence of the Secretary of State for Scotland from the 1970s until 1999. It has been the official residence of every First Minister since Donald Dewar assumed office in 1999.

pairs.question: What architectural style and materials were used in the construction of Bute House?
pairs.answer: Bute House was constructed in an 18th-century Neoclassical style using sandstone materials, reflecting the Georgian architectural heritage of Edinburgh.

pairs.question: What notable architectural significance does Bute House and Charlotte Square hold in Edinburgh?
pairs.answer: Together with Charlotte Square as a whole, Bute House has been described as 'perhaps the finest architectural achievement of Georgian Edinburgh,' showcasing the work of Robert Adam and James Craig.

pairs.question: What are some of the key functions and rooms within Bute House?
pairs.answer: Bute House serves multiple functions, including acting as the official residence of the First Minister, hosting press conferences, cabinet meetings (held in the Cabinet Room every Tuesday), and meetings of the Scottish Government. It also contains ministerial offices, conference rooms, reception areas, sitting and dining rooms, as well as the First Minister's private residence on the second and third floors.
